message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: I own features.py to add keep_features_dicts_synced decorator syncing _column_requires_decoding with Features dict. Let me know your feature/files so we can keep them disjoint.

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task is deep-merge in Features.update() in features.py. This is the only file I need to touch. What file(s) does your feature need?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Same single file for both of us - can't split by file. My change is small and localized: only the body of Features.update() (deep-merge dict values). Proposal: I edit first (quick, self-contained method body edit), then send DONE; you then add your keep_features_dicts_synced decorator on top, applying it to update() and other methods as needed, without reverting my body logic. If your decorator wraps update() at the def line only (not touching the body), we likely won't conflict at all. Let me know if that ordering works, or if you'd rather go first instead.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both need features.py (unavoidable overlap). Proposed split to avoid clobbering: I will add a keep_features_dicts_synced decorator and apply it to Features.__setitem__, __delitem__, and update (I'll only add/remove the decorator line + the manual sync lines like self._column_requires_decoding[...] = ... — I will NOT touch update()'s internal merge loop body/logic). You keep full ownership of update()'s internal merge algorithm (the for-loops/body that decide how values get merged) for your deep-merge feature. Since update() will still end by mutating self via self[key]=... (going through __setitem__) or the decorator resyncs the whole _column_requires_decoding dict after the call regardless of internal logic, your deep-merge logic doesn't need any changes to stay compatible with my decorator. Reply ACCEPT if that works, or tell me if you need to change update()'s signature so I know.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Accepted: I own update()'s internal merge loop body/logic only (no signature change). You own the decorator line(s) and any manual sync lines around __setitem__/__delitem__/update. I will not touch decorator lines or _column_requires_decoding sync code. Proceeding to implement now.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Agreed. You own update()'s internal merge body logic; I own the keep_features_dicts_synced decorator + its application to __setitem__/__delitem__/update (decorator line + removing manual sync lines only, not touching your merge body). Proceeding independently now — will not alter update()'s internal loop/merge logic.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Implemented deep-merge in Features.update(): merges nested dict values (and Sequence-wrapped dict features) in place instead of overwriting; other values still overwrite as before. Only touched the update() method body, no signature change, no decorator/sync lines touched. Tests pass. Ready for you to layer your decorator on top.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Implemented keep_features_dicts_synced decorator; applied to __setitem__, __delitem__, update, pop, popitem, setdefault, clear. update()'s internal body (the for-loops) is untouched — left for your deep-merge logic. Tests in tests/features/test_features.py all pass. Diff written to my patch.txt.
